description:
You are a junior log–analyst who has just received
    /home/user/logs/access.log – a 30-line Apache “combined” style
    access log.  
    Using **only awk and/or sed (plus shell redirection, sort, uniq, etc.)**
    perform the investigation steps below and write every report to the
    exact path that is given.  
    Nothing in the reports may contain extra header lines, blank lines,
    tab-characters or trailing spaces — automated tests compare the
    files byte-for-byte.

    1. 404 traffic per IP  
       • Scan the log for requests whose **status code is 404**.  
       • Count how many 404s each **client IP** generated.  
       • Save the result to /home/user/analysis/404_by_ip.txt  
         Format & sort order (strict):  
         “<ip> <count>” (single space separator),  
         sort numerically descending by the count; when two counts are
         equal sort the IPs in ascending dotted-decimal order.  
         Example of the required layout:  
         127.0.0.1 42⏎

    2. Top 10 requested endpoints  
       • Ignore the HTTP method and HTTP version – just extract the
         “path” portion that starts with “/”.  
       • Count how many times each path appears in the whole log,
         regardless of status code.  
       • Write the ten most frequently requested paths to
         /home/user/analysis/top10_endpoints.txt  
         Format: “<endpoint> <count>”  
         Sort order: descending by count, ties broken by lexicographic
         (byte) order on the path itself.

    3. Overall status-code distribution  
       • Produce a CSV file called /home/user/analysis/status_codes.csv  
         Exact format:  
         Line-1   status,count  
         Line-N   <status>,<total for that status>  
         Rows must be ordered by the **numeric value of the status code
         in ascending order** (e.g. 200 before 404).  
         There must be no extra spaces anywhere in the file.

    4. Sanitised copy of the log  
       • Create /home/user/logs/access_sanitized.log which is identical to
         the original, except that every literal substring  
         “GET /old-page” is replaced by “GET /new-page”.  
       • All other characters, spacing and line order must remain
         untouched.

    A quick checklist for the automated grader  
    • /home/user/analysis/404_by_ip.txt exists, has exactly 6 lines.  
    • /home/user/analysis/top10_endpoints.txt exists, has exactly 10
      lines.  
    • /home/user/analysis/status_codes.csv exists, has header +
      exactly 5 data lines.  
    • /home/user/logs/access_sanitized.log exists, still has 30 lines,
      and the six occurrences of “GET /old-page” are now
      “GET /new-page”.  

    Work solely in the terminal; do **not** delete or rename the
    original /home/user/logs/access.log.
